---
title: "5.3.1 Cyber security threats"
subtitle: "Social engineering · P196-P198"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .figure-placeholder { border: 1px dashed #8aa0bf; border-radius: 8px; background: #f4f8ff; color: #40526b; padding: 1.1em 1.3em; margin: 0.8em 0; font-size: 0.9em; text-align: center; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.68em; line-height: 1.06; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.2em 0.3em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)

## Social Engineering

### Manipulating people

<span class="term">Social engineering</span> (社会工程) occurs when a cybercriminal creates a social situation that can lead to a potential victim dropping their guard.

It involves the manipulation of people into breaking their normal security procedures and not following best practice.

## Figure 5.11

### Five common threats 1/2

::: {.clean-table}
| Threat | Textbook idea |
|---|---|
| Instant messaging | malicious links are embedded into instant messages, for example an important software upgrade; relies on curiosity |
| Scareware | a pop-up claims the computer is infected and the user needs to download fake anti-virus immediately; relies on fear |
| Emails/phishing scams | the user is tricked by a genuine-looking email and opens a link to a fake website; relies on trust |
:::

## Figure 5.11

### Five common threats 2/2

::: {.clean-table}
| Threat | Textbook idea |
|---|---|
| Baiting | a malware-infected memory stick is left where it can be found; relies on curiosity |
| Phone calls | a so-called IT professional claims the device has been compromised and asks the user to download software; relies on fear |
:::

## Human Emotions

### Fear, curiosity, empathy and trust

The whole idea is based on the exploitation of certain human emotions.

The three most common ones to exploit are:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">fear</span>: the user is panicked into believing their computer is in immediate danger
- <span class="term">curiosity</span>: the user is tricked into following a tempting claim or opening a found memory stick
- <span class="term">empathy and trust</span>: genuine-sounding companies are assumed to be safe
:::

## No Hacking Involved

### The user allows access

There is no hacking involved.

The user is willingly allowing the cybercriminal to have access to their computer, to download malicious software or to visit fake websites.

The user is rushed into making rash decisions.

## Figure 5.12

### Stages in a typical scam

::: {.figure-placeholder}
Figure 5.12 Stages in a typical social engineering scam screenshot placeholder
:::

## Social Engineering Stages

### Stage 1 and Stage 2

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Stage 1</span>: victims are identified; information about the victim is gathered and the method of attack is decided
- <span class="term">Stage 2</span>: the victim is targeted through email, phone call, Trojan horse and so on, depending on who the victim is
:::

## Social Engineering Stages

### Stage 3 and Stage 4

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">Stage 3</span>: the attack is executed, allowing the cybercriminal to obtain information or cause the disruption chosen at Stage 1
- <span class="term">Stage 4</span>: when the cybercriminal has what they wanted, they try to remove traces of the malware to cover their tracks
:::

## Classroom Check

### Social engineering

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- social engineering manipulates people into breaking normal security procedures
- examples include instant messaging, scareware, phishing emails, baiting and phone calls
- it exploits fear, curiosity, empathy and trust
- no hacking is involved because the user willingly allows access or downloads malware
- the scam stages are identify victim, target victim, execute attack and cover tracks
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Keeping data safe

The next section moves from threats to solutions that keep data safe.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 5 Directory / 返回 Unit 5 目录](../../index.html)
